metadata.description: Synthetic chromosome aberration / breakpoint interaction data for prostate cancer graph modeling, based on biological priors from chromoplexy and AMG literature. No copy-number alterations included. Data are fully synthetic and not derived from any real patient.
biological_priors_consulted: Ensembl/NCBI gene annotations for prostate cancer genes
Literature on chromoplexy and chromothripsis in prostate cancer
metadata.disclaimer: No live databases were queried. All patterns are synthesized from known biological mechanisms and the attached papers as conceptual guidance. This data is for methodological testing only.

generation_assumptions.breakpoint_distribution: Non-uniform; clustered in known prostate cancer gene regions and randomly elsewhere; interchromosomal fraction higher in ETS+ adenocarcinoma-like cases; intrachromosomal clustered rearrangements higher in CHD1-deletion-like NEPC cases.
generation_assumptions.chain_formation: Based on chromoplexy model: breakpoints within a chain are non-independent, often forming closed cycles (deletion bridges or adjacency cycles).
generation_assumptions.cycle_structure_compatibility: Cycles in the interaction graph correspond to even-length cycles in AMG terms; each cycle represents a coordinated multi-break event.
generation_assumptions.held_out_targets: One true interaction per case is masked to serve as prediction benchmark; the interaction is selected from a cycle to test graph completion.
generation_assumptions.no_cnv: No copy number alterations, amplifications, deletions, or segmented CNV tracks are included. Only structural breakpoints and interactions.
generation_assumptions.disease_type_differences: Primary adenocarcinoma: more interchromosomal chains, frequent involvement of ETS fusion-like patterns (e.g., TMPRSS2-ERG mimic). NEPC: more intrachromosomal clustered breaks, higher breakpoint density, CHD1-like loss patterns, fewer interchromosomal links.

interactions.support_rationale: Fusion of TMPRSS2 and ERG via deletion bridge; common in prostate cancer.
Interchromosomal fusion linking chr21 to chr7; part of chromoplexy chain.
Interchromosomal fusion connecting chr7 to chr10; PTEN region involved.
Deletion spanning PTEN exons; loss of tumor suppressor.
Interchromosomal fusion to chr5; part of chain closure.
Intrachromosomal adjacency on chr5; breakpoint cluster.
Interchromosomal fusion to chr18; SMAD4 region.
Cycle closure: back to B1, completing chromoplexy chain.

held_out_targets.reason_for_holdout: Masked for graph prediction benchmark; true fusion present in full data.

interactions.support_rationale: Intrachromosomal fusion near MYC; possible amplification effect without CNV.
Cycle closure linking back to chr8 breakpoint.

held_out_targets.reason_for_holdout: Held out for graph edge prediction.

interactions.support_rationale: Deletion spanning CHD1 locus; typical in NEPC.

held_out_targets.reason_for_holdout: Masked for prediction; part of chromothripsis-like cycle.
Held out for graph completion benchmark.

interactions.support_rationale: Clustered breaks in gene-poor region on chr3.

held_out_targets.reason_for_holdout: Masked to test prediction of deletion bridge.
Held out for interchromosomal edge prediction.

interactions.support_rationale: Return fusion to chr12; no cycle closure (linear path).

held_out_targets.reason_for_holdout: Masked for high-density breakpoint graph completion.

interactions.support_rationale: Interchromosomal fusion linking PTEN and RB1 regions.
Return fusion; linear path, no cycle.

held_out_targets.reason_for_holdout: Masked for simple interchromosomal fusion prediction.

interactions.support_rationale: Return fusion; linear path no cycle.
Interchromosomal link to chr17 TP53 region.
Cycle closure; weak evidence due to distance.

comparability_notes.spirit_comparability: Data are comparable in spirit to the chromoplexy chains described in Baca et al. 2013 and the AMG cycle structures in Sheth et al. 2026. The synthetic cases include chained rearrangements with deletion bridges, interchromosomal and intrachromosomal fusions, and closed cycles (analogous to even-length cycles in AMGs). The distribution of breakpoints and interactions reflects known prostate cancer patterns: ETS+ adenocarcinomas show higher interchromosomal fractions, while NEPC cases show clustered intrachromosomal rearrangements reminiscent of CHD1-loss patterns. No copy-number data are included, focusing purely on structural breakpoint interactions.
comparability_notes.differences_from_real_data: Real patient data would include exact genomic coordinates, often with additional complex features such as phased breakpoints, precise sequence microhomology, and copy-number changes at breakpoints. The synthetic data uses approximate positions and simplified confidence values. The chain detection here is simplified; real ChainFinder analysis would incorporate statistical modeling of breakpoint distances and local rearrangement rates. Additionally, real tumors often have admixed normal cells and subclonal heterogeneity, which are not modeled. The held-out targets are randomly selected rather than based on true statistical uncertainty.
comparability_notes.intended_use: This synthetic data is suitable for early-stage methodological testing of graph-based models for missing interaction prediction, cycle detection, and graph completion. It should not be used for clinical inference or biological discovery without extensive validation against real whole-genome sequencing data. Error propagation studies should consider the simplified assumptions and lack of biological covariance.

assumptions_based_on_general_priors: Interchromosomal fraction differences between ETS+ and ETS- tumors are approximated; real variability may be higher.
NEPC pattern of intrachromosomal clustering is based on limited literature; actual NEPC genomes may show more interchromosomal events.
Breakpoint positions are randomly perturbed within gene regions; real breakpoints cluster non-randomly at fragile sites.
Confidence values are synthetic and not derived from real read support or statistical tests.
underdetermined_aspects: True rates of closed cycles in real prostate cancer genomes are not known; synthetic data may over- or under-represent them.
The relationship between chain length and clinical parameters is not modeled.
Subclonal architecture and timing of chromoplexy events are omitted.
possible_biases: Synthetic generation favors clean cycles and chains, which may be easier to predict than real fragmented data.
Held-out targets are selected without reference to true prediction difficulty; real missing data often arise from low coverage or complex rearrangements.
Overrepresentation of known cancer genes may inflate biological plausibility in a non-random way.
cautious_treatment_advice: Do not use these confidence values as probabilities of true biological interactions; treat them as relative weights for algorithm benchmarking.
When comparing synthetic to real data, expect higher noise, missing breakpoints, and non-cycle structures in real data.
Error propagation from synthetic to real data may underestimate prediction error because synthetic data lack biological covariances and measurement noise.
Validation on at least one real whole-genome sequencing dataset is strongly recommended before clinical or biological interpretation.
required_comparisons_before_trust: Compare synthetic breakpoint distance distributions to real prostate cancer WGS data.
Validate that graph-based models trained on synthetic data achieve similar performance on real data with held-out rearrangements.
Assess whether the synthetic interchromosomal/intrachromosomal ratios match real cohorts (e.g., TCGA-PRAD).
Test if synthetic closed cycles correspond to real chromoplexy cycles validated by long-read sequencing.
